summary:
The Andronovo culture was a broad Bronze Age archaeological horizon of the Eurasian steppe and forest-steppe, spanning from the Ural Mountains to the Yenisei River and from the taiga edge to the northern Central Asian steppes, dated approximately 2000–1200 BC. Rather than a single unified culture, it represents a family of related but distinct regional cultures — including the Fedorovo, Alakul, and Sintashta phases — that shared common features in pottery styles, burial customs, and bronze metallurgy. The Andronovo peoples were semi-nomadic pastoralists who herded cattle, sheep, and horses, supplemented by farming in favorable locations.

Andronovo communities were among the most important carriers of early Indo-Iranian cultural and genetic material across the steppe zone. Modern ancient DNA studies confirm that Andronovo-related populations contributed significantly to the gene pools of both South Asian populations via the [[event:1849|Indo-Aryan migrations]] and Central Asian populations that became the ancestors of Iranian-speaking peoples. The Andronovo archaeological horizon thus represents a key intermediary phase in one of the most consequential population movements in human prehistory.

Andronovo bronze metallurgy was highly developed, producing weapons, ornaments, and tools distributed across vast steppe networks. Burial evidence shows a stratified society with an elite associated with horses, weapons, and chariots, as in the closely related [[event:1860|Sintashta culture]]. The gradual transition from Andronovo to successor cultures by around 1200 BC coincided with the broader transformations associated with climatic shifts and the movements of peoples that characterized the end of the Bronze Age across Eurasia.
